epigraph.text: For as the heavens are higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than your ways and my thoughts than your thoughts.

content.text:
When chords, secants, and tangents intersect, they create segments 
with fascinating relationships. These relationships, sometimes called 
the "Power of a Point" theorems, have been known since antiquity and 
find applications in optics, navigation, and computer graphics. The 
underlying principle is that certain products of segment lengths remain 
constant—an idea that connects geometry to algebra in a beautiful way.
Intersecting Chords

When two chords intersect inside a circle, they divide each other 
into segments. The products of these segments have a surprising property.

content.title: Chord-Chord Product Theorem
content.content:
If two chords intersect inside a circle, then the product of the 
segments of one chord equals the product of the segments of the other:
\[
 AP \cdot PB = CP \cdot PD
\]
where $P$ is the point of intersection.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: *Proof:* Draw $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$.

Inscribed angles $\angle ACP$ and $\angle DBP$ both intercept arc $AD$, 
so $\angle ACP = \angle DBP$ (inscribed angles intercepting the same arc).

Similarly, $\angle CAP$ and $\angle BDP$ both intercept arc $BC$, 
so $\angle CAP = \angle BDP$.
By AA Similarity, $\triangle APC \sim \triangle DPB$.

Therefore:
\[\frac{AP}{DP} = \frac{CP}{BP}\]

Cross-multiplying: $AP \cdot BP = CP \cdot DP$, or equivalently 
$AP \cdot PB = CP \cdot PD$.

content.title: Finding a segment using intersecting chords
content.solution:
By the Chord-Chord Product Theorem:
$$
 4 \cdot 6 &= 3 \cdot x \\
 24 &= 3x \\
 x &= 8
$$

content.title: Intersecting chords with algebra
content.problem:
Two chords intersect inside a circle at point $P$. One chord has 
segments $2x$ and $x + 3$, and the other has segments $x + 1$ and 
$x + 5$. Find $x$ and the lengths of all segments.
content.solution:
By the Chord-Chord Product Theorem:
$$
 (2x)(x + 3) &= (x + 1)(x + 5) \\
 2x^2 + 6x &= x^2 + 6x + 5 \\
 2x^2 &= x^2 + 5 \\
 x^2 &= 5 \\
 x &= \sqrt{5} \quad \text{(taking positive root)}
$$

The segments are:

- $2x = 2\sqrt{5}$

- $x + 3 = \sqrt{5} + 3$

- $x + 1 = \sqrt{5} + 1$

- $x + 5 = \sqrt{5} + 5$

We can verify: $(2\sqrt{5})(\sqrt{5} + 3) = 10 + 6\sqrt{5}$ and 
$(\sqrt{5} + 1)(\sqrt{5} + 5) = 5 + 5\sqrt{5} + \sqrt{5} + 5 = 10 + 6\sqrt{5}$

content.text: Secants from an External Point

When two secants are drawn from the same external point, a similar 
product relationship holds.

content.title: Secant Segment
content.content:
A **secant segment** is a segment from 
an external point through a circle, ending at the far intersection 
point. The **external segment** is the part outside the circle.

content.title: Secant-Secant Product Theorem
content.content:
If two secants are drawn from the same external point, then the 
product of one secant segment and its external segment equals the 
product of the other secant segment and its external segment:
\[
 PA \cdot PB = PC \cdot PD
\]
where $P$ is the external point, and $A$, $B$ are on one secant 
while $C$, $D$ are on the other.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: *Proof:* Draw $\overline{AD}$ and $\overline{CB}$.
$\angle APD = \angle CPB$ (same angle at $P$).

$\angle PAD$ and $\angle PCB$ both are inscribed angles intercepting 
arc $BD$, so $\angle PAD = \angle PCB$.
By AA Similarity, $\triangle PAD \sim \triangle PCB$.
Therefore: $\frac{PA}{PC} = \frac{PD}{PB}$
Cross-multiplying: $PA \cdot PB = PC \cdot PD$.

content.title: Secant-secant problem
content.solution:
First secant: external = 4, whole = $4 + 6 = 10$.

Second secant: external = 3, whole = $3 + x$ for some unknown 
chord length $x$.

By the Secant-Secant Product Theorem:
$$
 4 \cdot 10 &= 3 \cdot (3 + x) \\
 40 &= 9 + 3x \\
 31 &= 3x \\
 x &= \frac{31}{3}
$$

The whole second secant is $3 + \frac{31}{3} = \frac{40}{3} \approx 13.33$.

content.text: Secant and Tangent from an External Point

When a secant and a tangent are drawn from the same external point, 
another product relationship emerges.

content.title: Secant-Tangent Product Theorem
content.content:
If a secant and a tangent are drawn from the same external point, 
then the square of the tangent segment equals the product of the 
secant segment and its external segment:
\[
 (PT)^2 = PA \cdot PB
\]
where $T$ is the point of tangency, and $A$, $B$ are the intersection 
points of the secant with the circle.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
*Proof:* This can be viewed as a limiting case of the Secant-Secant 
Theorem. As one secant rotates toward the tangent position, its two 
intersection points merge into the single point of tangency $T$. The 
product $PC \cdot PD$ becomes $PT \cdot PT = (PT)^2$.

Alternatively, draw $\overline{TB}$ and $\overline{TA}$. Show 
$\triangle PTA \sim \triangle PTB$ using the inscribed angle $\angle TBA$ 
and the tangent-chord angle $\angle PTA$.

content.title: Secant-tangent problem
content.problem:
A tangent from point $P$ to a circle has length 6. A secant from 
$P$ has an external segment of 4. Find the length of the internal 
segment (chord portion) of the secant.
content.solution:
Let the whole secant be $4 + x$ where $x$ is the internal segment.

By the Secant-Tangent Product Theorem:
$$
 6^2 &= 4 \cdot (4 + x) \\
 36 &= 16 + 4x \\
 20 &= 4x \\
 x &= 5
$$

The internal segment has length 5.

content.title: Finding the tangent length
content.problem:
From external point $P$, a secant is drawn with external segment 3 
and whole length 12. A tangent from $P$ touches the circle at $T$. 
Find $PT$.
content.solution:
By the Secant-Tangent Product Theorem:
$$
 (PT)^2 &= 3 \cdot 12 \\
 (PT)^2 &= 36 \\
 PT &= 6
$$

content.text: The Power of a Point

All three theorems in this section are special cases of a single, 
unifying concept.

content.title: Power of a Point
content.content:
The **power of a point** $P$ with 
respect to a circle with center $O$ and radius $r$ is defined as:
\[
 \text{pow}(P) = PO^2 - r^2
\]

content.text:
The remarkable fact is that if any line through $P$ intersects the 
circle at points $A$ and $B$, then:
\[
 PA \cdot PB = |\text{pow}(P)|
\]

This product is the same for *every* line through $P$—it 
depends only on the point's position relative to the circle.

content.title: Using the power of a point
content.problem:
Point $P$ is 10 units from the center of a circle with radius 6. 
Find the power of $P$. Then find the length of a tangent from $P$ 
to the circle.
content.solution:
The power of $P$ is:
\[\text{pow}(P) = PO^2 - r^2 = 100 - 36 = 64\]

For a tangent from $P$, touching the circle at $T$:
\[(PT)^2 = \text{pow}(P) = 64\]
\[PT = 8\]

content.text: Angles Formed by Secants and Tangents

When secants or tangents intersect, the angle formed can be 
computed from the intercepted arcs.

content.title: Angle Formed by Two Chords
content.content:
The measure of an angle formed by two chords intersecting inside 
a circle is half the sum of the intercepted arcs:
\[
 m\angle 1 = \frac{1}{2}(m\widearc{AC} + m\widearc{BD})
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Angle Formed by Two Secants (External)
content.content:
The measure of an angle formed by two secants, two tangents, or a 
secant and a tangent drawn from an external point is half the 
**difference** of the intercepted arcs:
\[
 m\angle P = \frac{1}{2}(m\widearc{AC} - m\widearc{BD})
\]
where $\widearc{AC}$ is the larger intercepted arc.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Angle formed by secants
content.problem:
Two secants from external point $P$ intercept arcs of $100°$ and 
$40°$ on the circle. Find the angle at $P$.

content.text: Exercises

marginNotes.text:
The heavens declare the glory of God, 
and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.

He stretches out the north over the void 
and hangs the earth on nothing.

He has made everything beautiful 
in its time. Also, he has put eternity into man's heart.
